sections.title: Implementation
sections.content: 8.1 Feature Representation The effectiveness of surrogate models, particularly parametric ones, often hinges on the choice of feature representation. In scientiﬁc domains, domain-speciﬁc representations can dramatically improve model performance. For example, in chemistry, molecular graphs can be embedded using graph neural networks, descriptors, or ﬁngerprints; in biology, protein sequences may be represented using sequence embeddings or structural features. The choice of representation should reﬂect the relevant properties of the domain and the nature of the optimisation task. 8.2 Hyperparameter Adaptation Surrogate models, especially GPs, involve several hyperparameters, including those governing the kernel (e.g., lengthscale, variance), the mean function, and the noise model. The choice of hyperparameters signiﬁcantly affects the surrogates predictive accuracy and uncertainty quantiﬁcation. 8.2.1 Maximising the Marginal Likelihood principled approach to hyperparameter selection is to maximise the marginal likelihood, which is the probability of the observed data under the model, integrating over the latent function values. For surrogate model with hyperparameters λ, this means λ = arg max p(y x, λ) (23) λ where p(y x, λ) is the likelihood of the observed data given the hyperparameters. This approach applies to any surrogate model with hyperparameters. Taking the GP surrogate (see Section 7.2) for example, given observations with i.i.d. additive Gaussian noise, Eq. (23) becomes maximizing: log p(y x, λ) = (cid:0) 1 2 (cid:0) (y (cid:0) m) Kxx + σ2I (cid:1)1 (y (cid:0) m) (cid:0) 1 2 (cid:0) log det Kxx + σ2I (cid:1) (cid:0) 2 log(2π), where is the dimension (length) of y. 8.2.2 Hierarchical Bayes Alternatively, fully Bayesian treatment places priors over the hyperparameters and integrates them out, yielding hierarchical model. In the Bayesian framework, the predictive distribution for new input ˆx is p(ˆy ˆx, x, y) = p(ˆy ˆx, x, y, λ) p(λ x, y) dλ, where p(λ x, y) is the posterior over hyperparameters. In practice, this integration is often intractable and is approximated using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) or variational inference. Hierarchical Bayes provides more robust uncertainty estimates, particularly when data is scarce or hyperparameters are poorly identiﬁed.

sections.title: 9 Coding the Surrogate Model
sections.content: This section provides practical introduction to surrogate modelling within Bayesian Optimization (BO) software, focusing on Gaussian Process (GP) models. First, we show how surrogate models are typically implemented and conﬁgured in BO frameworks, exempliﬁed by HEBO. Furthermore, we demonstrate how to create and customize Gaussian Process surrogates from scratch using two popular Python libraries. The coding patterns we present are invariant across applications and problem settings, so we do not provide case-by-case studies. 9.1 Surrogate Modelling in BO Software Most BO software naturally employs Gaussian Processes (see Section 7) as surrogate models, where the hyperparameters are inferred from the available data. Through the software interface, users can typically conﬁgure the surrogate model by passing speciﬁc arguments; however, the degree of customization is often limited by the interface design. In the following, we use HEBO as an illustrative example. 29 1 3 4 5 6 7 9 10 11 12

sections.title: BO Tutorial
sections.content: from hebo . optimizers . hebo import HEBO from hebo . design_space . design_space import DesignSpace # Define the design space ( example ) space = DesignSpace () . parse ({ " name " : " x1 " , " type " : " num " , " lb " : 0 , " ub " : 1 }) # Instantiate HEBO with specific surrogate model opt = HEBO ( space = space , model_name = " gp " , model_config ={ " lr " : 0.01 , " num_epochs " : 100} # Surrogate model name ( default : Gaussian Process ) # Configuration for the surrogate . ) For the Gaussian Process, which is the default surrogate, HEBO automatically constructs kernel suitable for heterogeneous variables. Speciﬁcally, it employs Matern kernel for numeric variables and combines it with appropriate kernels for categorical variables when necessary. Kernel hyperparameters, such as lengthscales, are initialized based on the dataset. This design is practical for most scenarios, offering robust and ﬂexible surrogate without requiring users to specify detailed conﬁgurations. HEBOs GP implementation is powered by GPyTorch [25], which ﬁts kernel and mean function hyperparameters from data. Additionally, HEBO enables users to customize the GP further by passing dictionary via model_config. For example, one can specify the learning rate (lr) and the number of epochs (num_epochs) for hyperparameter optimization. Detailed conﬁguration of the GP in HEBO is beyond the scope of this tutorial2, and the default HEBO settings are generally suitable for most use cases without additional conﬁguration. HEBO also supports variety of non-GP surrogate models. For instance, rf (Random Forest) is suitable for nonsmooth objective functions; deep_ensemble, psgld, and mcbn are neural network-based models designed for probabilistic regression [54, 95]. Moreover, HEBO offers specialized models such as gumbel, masked_deep_ensemble, and fe_deep_ensemble, which incorporate feature selection mechanisms. While this tutorial does not delve into the technical details of these surrogatestopics that require extensive machine learning backgroundit is useful to acknowledge their existence. This awareness enables users to tackle broader range of optimization problems beyond the scope of traditional Gaussian Processes. 9.2 Creating Your Own Gaussian Processes The ﬂexibility of surrogate models within BO software is often limited by the interface and typically encapsulated deep within the implementation, making it difﬁcult to inspect or customize. However, it is possible to fully examine, control, and even tailor surrogate modelling. In this section, we demonstrate how to achieve this in Python. Speciﬁcally, we introduce two librariesScikit-Learn [70] and GPyTorch [25]to create Gaussian Process surrogates (see Section 7) for Bayesian optimization. Scikit-Learn is well-suited for small to moderate-sized datasets and rapid prototyping due to its uniﬁed interface and ease of use. It abstracts many technical details, allowing researchers to focus on modelling concepts rather than implementation intricacies. Conversely, GPyTorch is designed for scalability and customizability. It supports GPU-accelerated inference and training, making it ideal for large-scale or high-dimensional problems. Furthermore, GPyTorch offers ﬁne-grained control over GP architectures, kernel composition, and training procedures, which is essential for advanced research and engineering applications. This ﬂexibility, however, comes with increased complexity and steeper learning curve. This tutorial serves as an introductory guide, aiming to provide conceptual clarity and practical orientation rather than exhaustive technical coverage. The code examples presented are deliberately minimal and intended for illustration only. Readers with strong interest in Gaussian Process modelling or those seeking to deploy GPs in production or at scale are encouraged to consult the ofﬁcial documentation and community resources for Scikit-Learn (https: //scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/gaussian_process.html) and GPyTorch (https://gpytorch.ai/). Both libraries are actively maintained and offer extensive examples, API references, and tutorials. Replicating such comprehensive material here is neither practical nor desirable; instead, our focus is to introduce fundamental concepts and direct readers to authoritative sources for further study. 2For readers interested in in-depth customization of GP surrogates and possessing relevant coding expertise, we refer to the HEBO source code at https://github.com/huawei-noah/HEBO/tree/master/HEBO/hebo.

This design abstracts much of the complexity of GP inference, allowing users to focus on model selection and interpretation rather than implementation details. The interface is consistent with other Scikit-Learn estimators, facilitating integration into broader machine learning workﬂows. sections.title:
Part III
Acting and Decision Making
sections.content: The concept of decision making can be viewed as mapping from knowledge to action. In the context of scientiﬁc discovery, decision making translates our current beliefs about hypothesis into actionsspeciﬁcally, the selection of subsequent experiments. The overarching aim is to acquire more knowledge about the unknown and to achieve higher objective values. In sequential optimisation, this process is formalised as policy π, which maps the accumulated data Dt to the design xt+1 2 for the next experiment. For both sequential model-based optimisation (SMBO) and Bayesian Optimisation (BO), this policy π determines the next action using the posterior model M(ˆf jˆx, Dt). In the sections that follow, we explore the concepts, theoretical foundations, and coding implementations that underlie the construction of such policy within the BO framework.

sections.title: 10 Acquisition Function
sections.content: Given surrogate model for the unknown objective : ! R, we now turn our attention to the crucial aspect of decision making: namely, given the current model M(ˆf jˆx, Dt), how should we select the next design xt+1? To address this question, BO reframes decision making as the optimisation of tractable, white-box function known as the acquisition function. 10.1 Motivation and Deﬁnition Experiments aimed at efﬁcient discovery must be designed according to sound principles. The central idea of BO aligns closely with the Bayesian philosophy of scientiﬁc discovery discussed in Section 1.1: experiments should be designed not only by leveraging our current knowledge, but also by acknowledging and incorporating the uncertainty arising from our ignorance [26]. In this context, the acquisition function acts as principled criterion for evaluating the potential utility of each candidate point, quantifying the expected beneﬁt of evaluating the objective at that point given the current surrogate model and observed data. It provides systematic approach to selecting the next experiment, inherently balancing the trade-off between exploration (gathering information in poorly understood regions) and exploitation (focusing on regions likely to yield high objective values). Deﬁnition 10.1 (Acquisition Function). The acquisition function, denoted α(xjD), is known function that can be efﬁciently computed from the surrogate model and the current data D. It assigns score to each candidate point 2 , reﬂecting the expected beneﬁt of evaluating (x) given the current knowledge. Given the acquisition function, the decision-making process becomes the maximisation of the acquisition function: xt+1 = arg max xX α(xjDt) (24) The acquisition function is constructed upon the surrogate model and is designed to be much less computationally demanding than evaluating the true objective function. In practice, this means that the computational cost associated with optimising the acquisition function is negligible compared to the expense of conducting real experiments. As result, the process of decision makingi.e., maximising the acquisition functionis far more tractable than directly optimising the costly, black-box objective. In essence, by maximising the acquisition function, we transform the complex problem of experimental design into manageable optimisation task. Furthermore, this approach is ﬁrmly grounded in the principles of Bayesian decision theory, which we discuss in greater detail in Section 11. 10.2 Trading off Exploration and Exploitation distinguishing feature of Bayesian Optimisation is that the surrogate model provides not only predictions but also uncertainty estimates, as result of Bayesian inference. This uncertainty can be strategically exploited to actively explore the unknown objective function. In this setting, decision making must strike careful balance between two competing objectives: exploration, which involves sampling points where the model is uncertain in order to reduce uncertainty and potentially discover superior solutions; and exploitation, which involves sampling points where the model predicts high objective values, thereby seeking to improve performance based on current knowledge. The acquisition function is speciﬁcally designed to mediate this explorationexploitation trade-off. Its mathematical form typically incorporates both the predicted mean and the uncertainty (variance) of the surrogate model at each

sections.title: BO Tutorial
sections.content: candidate point. By doing so, the acquisition function encourages sampling in regions that are either highly promising (exploitation) or highly uncertain (exploration). This dual focus enables BO to efﬁciently optimise the unknown objective by judiciously allocating experimental resources. 10.3 Common Choices of Acquisition Functions Figure 6: ReLU (u) & I(u) Maximising each of these functions implies encouraging their internal expressions to be positive. In the case of the Heaviside function, this corresponds to driving the argument above zero to trigger nonzero response. For the ReLU function, the goal is to make the argument as large and positive as possible to maximise the acquisition value. Common choices of acquisition functions in Bayesian optimisation encode different strategies for balancing exploration of uncertain regions and exploitation of promising areas suggested by the surrogate model. Popular choices include Probability of Improvement (PI), which prioritises regions likely to outperform the current best; Expected Improvement (EI), which accounts for both probability and magnitude of improvement; and Upper Conﬁdence Bound (UCB), which explicitly trades off mean prediction and uncertainty. More recent criteria, such as information-theoretic acquisition functions [100], are not included in this tutorial. Figure 7 illustrates typical shapes of these acquisition functions over one-dimensional input space. Expected Improvement (EI) The expected improvement has been one of the most commonly used acquisition functions since the early works of Mockus [62] and Jones, Schonlau, and Welch [42]: , αEI(x D) = Ef M(,D) (x) (cid:0) (x+) ReLU (cid:1)(cid:3) (cid:0) (cid:2) where x+ is the best performing design in the data so far. The ReLU function is deﬁned as ReLU(u) = maxf0, ug and is illustrated in Figure 6. Here, the subscript (cid:24) M((cid:1) (cid:1), D) indicates that the evaluation of complies with the posterior surrogate M(ˆf jˆx, D) given data D. Probability of Improvement (PI) PI is another classic acquisition function, ﬁrst introduced by Kushner [50]: αPI(x D) = Ef M(,D) (x) (cid:0) (x+) , (cid:2) (cid:0) (cid:1)(cid:3) where I(u) is the left-continuous Heaviside step function, (cid:26) 0 1 PI measures the probability that sampling at will yield an improvement over the current best observation (x+). While intuitive and straightforward, PI can be overly exploitative unless an improvement threshold is introduced. if (cid:20) 0, if > 0. I(u) = Upper Conﬁdence Bound (UCB) UCB is canonical acquisition function inspired by multi-armed bandit problems [2, 51]: αUCB(x D) = µ(x) + β σ(x), where µ(x) and σ(x) are the posterior mean and standard deviation of (x), which are estimated from the surrogate model at x. The parameter β > 0 controls the trade-off between exploration and exploitation: larger values of β encourage more exploration, while smaller values favour exploitation. UCB is appealing for its simplicity and strong theoretical guarantees in certain settings.

sections.title: BO Tutorial
sections.content: Figure 7: Visualization of the Gaussian Process (GP) surrogate model and acquisition functions used in Bayesian Optimization. The true objective function, (x) = sin(2πx), is shown as dashed black line. Observed data points are marked as red circles, where the Gaussian noise satisﬁes (0, 0.12). The GP surrogate model is depicted as the blue curve (mean prediction), with the shaded region representing the 95% conﬁdence interval (µ (cid:6) 2σ). The acquisition functionsUpper Conﬁdence Bound (UCB), Expected Improvement (EI), and Probability of Improvement (PI)are plotted in green, blue, and red, respectively, to illustrate their behaviour in guiding the selection of the next evaluation point. We do not plot Thompson Sampling as it is randomly drawn from the posterior surrogate. Thompson Sampling (TS) Thompson Sampling [76] is an alternative acquisition strategy rooted in bandit algorithms. At each iteration, one samples function from the posterior surrogate model, and selects the maximiser: αTS(x D) = (x), (cid:24) M((cid:1) (cid:1), D). Thompson Sampling inherently balances exploration and exploitation by sampling according to the models current uncertainty. Its conceptual simplicity and strong theoretical guarantees make it attractive, especially in parallel or distributed settings. It is important to note that the above acquisition functions are myopicthey consider only the next decision based on the current estimation of the objective, without anticipating the outcomes of future experiments. In reality, the current decision can inﬂuence subsequent decisions; thus, it may be desirable to consider the long-term effects of actions. However, Bayesian Optimisation typically employs these myopic acquisition functions, as they have been found to be sufﬁciently effective and computationally efﬁcient in practice. The rationale is that the surrogate model is itself an approximation of the true objective, so the cost of overly precise planningpredicting long-term effects given an approximate modeloften outweighs the potential beneﬁt. Nonetheless, non-myopic acquisition functions can be valuable in certain scenarios, particularly when the experimental budget is extremely limited (i.e., when only very small number of trials are possible). For more general and principled perspective on acquisition functions and decision making, we provide theoretical discussion in Section 11.

sections.title: 11 Bayesian Decision Theory for Optimisation
sections.content: This section provides the theoretical motivation and justiﬁcation for the acquisition function. 11.1 Utility and Bayesian Decision Making Given the entire optimisation history Dt := f(xi, yi)gt u(Dt+s). For example, typical utility is the best objective value ever observed after additional experiments: i=1, we deﬁne the utility as function of the s-step look-ahead, u(Dt+s) := max t+1it+s yi (cid:0) max 1it yi. Other possible utility functions may also consider the reduction in model uncertainty (information gain), which is the basis for Bayesian Experimental Design (BED) [73]. Since our aim here is to elucidate the mathematical foundation of acquisition functions, we do not cover the advanced design of utility functions in this tutorial. Bayesian decision making, given the model, requires us to choose xt+1 so as to maximise the expected posterior utility: xt+1 = arg max xX E[u(Dt+s)jDt, x] (25) This formulation makes explicit that future experiments are involved and must be predicted using the current model, taking into account both future model adaptation and the subsequent policy. In other words, the optimal decision

sections.title: BO Tutorial
sections.content: at each step depends on the expected outcomes of all future decisions and the evolution of the model as new data are collected. This recursive dependency is hallmark of sequential decision making and highlights the complexity inherent in optimal experimental design. 11.2 From Utility to Acquisition Function From Eq. 25, we see that decision making is, at its core, the optimisation of function, which we denote as α(xjDt) := E[u(Dt+s)jDt, x]. This function serves as the acquisition function, encapsulating the expected utility of each possible next action. Thus, the acquisition function is not arbitrary, but is directly motivated by the principle of maximising expected utility in Bayesian decision theory. Moreover, each commonly used acquisition function can be understood as an implicit treatment of particular choice of utility function, i.e., concrete way of quantifying the value of future data or decisions. Myopic Acquisition Function If the step look-ahead is = 1, the acquisition function simpliﬁes signiﬁcantly, as it considers only the immediate effect of the next experiment. This leads to the so-called myopic acquisition functions, which are widely used in practice due to their computational tractability. Myopic acquisition functions do not require explicit modelling of future decisions or model updates beyond the next experiment, making them both practical and efﬁcient. Non-myopic Acquisition Function If the look-ahead > 1, the acquisition function becomes substantially more challenging to compute, as it involves predicting future observations, updating the model, and accounting for subsequent decision-making behaviour. While non-myopic acquisition functions offer the potential advantage of more globally optimal planning [111], their implementation is often computationally intractable, even for two-step lookahead [104]. Various approximate methods have been proposed (e.g., [92, 30]), but these approaches are seldom used in practice due to their complexity and high computational cost. Nonetheless, in scenarios with extremely limited experimental budgets, non-myopic strategies may offer signiﬁcant beneﬁts by allowing more informed and farsighted planning.

sections.title: 12 Coding the Acquisition Function
sections.content: 12.1 Specifying Acquisition Functions in BO Software 1 2 3 4 6 7 8 9 10 The acquisition function (AF) plays central role in Bayesian Optimisation (BO) algorithms, as it determines the strategy by which the next evaluation points are selected. In practical software implementations, the choice of acquisition function is typically encapsulated within the optimiser interface, and may be set either by default or explicitly by the user. For example, in the HEBO framework, the acquisition function is selected automatically according to the optimiser class, but can also be customised by specifying the acquisition class and its conﬁguration. from hebo . optimizers . bo import BO from hebo . optimizers . hebo import HEBO # Construct your DesignSpace ( replace with actual space construction ) space = ... # . . DesignSpace () . parse (...) # BO : uses the default acquisition ( LCB , lower confidence bound , for minimisation ) opt_bo = BO ( space ) # HEBO : high - level optimizer ; by default uses MACE , multi - objective synthesis of EI , PI , and LCB opt_hebo = HEBO ( space ) The HEBO package also supports customisation of the acquisition function. HEBO supports variety of acquisition functions, each corresponding to different strategy for balancing exploration and exploitation. The main acquisition classes available in hebo.acquisitions.acq are as follows: LCB (Lower Conﬁdence Bound): This is the minimisation-oriented version of the popular UCB acquisition (see Section 10.3). In HEBO, LCB is deﬁned as µ(x) (cid:0) κσ(x), where µ(x) and σ(x) are the predictive mean and standard deviation at x, and κ is trade-off parameter. This encourages exploration of uncertain regions while seeking low objective values.

sections.title: BO Tutorial
sections.content: Mean: Selects points with the lowest predicted mean, i.e., pure exploitation without consideration of uncertainty. Sigma: Selects points with the highest predictive uncertainty, i.e., pure exploration. EI (Expected Improvement): Measures the expected improvement (see Section 10.3) over the current best observation, balancing exploitation and exploration in principled manner. logEI: The logarithm of the expected improvement, which can be more numerically stable in some cases. WEI and Log_WEI: Weighted expected improvement and its logarithmic variant, which can be used for multiobjective or constrained settings. MES (Max-value Entropy Search): Focuses on reducing the uncertainty about the location of the global minimum [100]. MOMeanSigmaLCB: multi-objective acquisition that considers mean, standard deviation, and LCB jointly. MACE: The default in HEBO, this is multi-objective acquisition function that synthesises several canonical criteria (EI, PI, LCB) into uniﬁed acquisition, thus balancing multiple explorationexploitation strategies. It is important to note that in HEBO, all acquisition functions are implemented for minimisation tasks. For example, LCB is the lower conﬁdence bound, as opposed to the upper conﬁdence bound more commonly used for maximisation. The following code demonstrates how to instantiate HEBO or BO with explicit acquisition classes, including canonical single-objective functions such as EI, and the multi-objective MACE: from hebo . optimizers . bo import BO from hebo . optimizers . hebo import HEBO from hebo . acquisitions . acq import LCB , Mean , Sigma , EI , MACE , GeneralAcq space = ... # your DesignSpace # 1) Use LCB ( lower confidence bound ) with custom kappa value for exploration - exploitation balance opt_bo_lcb = BO ( space , acq_cls = LCB , acq_conf ={ kappa : 3.0}) # 2) Use Mean ( pure exploitation ) or Sigma ( pure exploration ) opt_bo_mean opt_bo_sigma = BO ( space , acq_cls = Sigma ) = BO ( space , acq_cls = Mean ) # 3) Use Expected Improvement ( EI ) , canonical acquisition function opt_bo_ei = BO ( space , acq_cls = EI ) # 4) Use MACE , the multi - objective synthesis acquisition ( default for HEBO ) opt_hebo_mace = HEBO ( space , acq_cls = MACE ) 1 2 3 4 5 7 8 9 10 11 13 14 15 16 17 12.2 Implementing Acquisition Functions from Your Surrogate Models In this section, we demonstrate how to implement common acquisition functionsExpected Improvement (EI), Probability of Improvement (PI), and Upper Conﬁdence Bound (UCB)using the Gaussian Process surrogates constructed in Section 9.2. These acquisition functions are popular to Bayesian optimisation, as discussed mathematically in Section 10.3. The code examples below build directly on the GP implementations using Scikit-Learn and GPyTorch; thus, we focus on the acquisition function logic, referencing the surrogate model code as needed. 12.2.1 Acquisition Functions for Scikit-Learn Gaussian Process https://github.com/zwyu-ai/BO-Tutorial-for-Sci/blob/main/coding_illustrations/gp_bo_ sklearn.ipynb Given ﬁtted GaussianProcessRegressor, the posterior mean µ(x) and standard deviation σ(x) at candidate points can be obtained using the predict method with return_std=True. The following code implements UCB, EI, and PI as Python functions, which can be evaluated at any candidate points:

sections.title: BO Tutorial
sections.content: Part IV Algorithmic Workﬂow and Implementation This part is dedicated to systematic exposition of the workﬂow, implementation, and practical extensions of Bayesian Optimisation (BO), building upon the foundational concepts established in earlier sections: speciﬁcally, the framing of scientiﬁc discovery as black-box optimisation problem, the design and use of surrogate models, and sequential decision-making via acquisition functions. The organisation of this part follows logical progression from core algorithmic principles to real-world scientiﬁc applications. First, we present compact yet comprehensive overview of the elementary BO workﬂow, detailing its iterative loop and key considerations for each sub-module. Next, we focus on the canonical GP-UCB algorithma widely studied instantiation of BOand formalise its theoretical guarantees (i.e., sub-linear regret). Subsequently, we address the distinctive challenges of BO in scientiﬁc discovery by reviewing technical extensions tailored to real experimental settings. We then translate these concepts into actionable code, providing implementations using state-ofthe-art BO libraries (e.g., HEBO) as well as custom workﬂows built from scratch with Gaussian process frameworks (Scikit-Learn and GPyTorch). Finally, we experimentally validate the efﬁcacy of BO on four representative scientiﬁcdiscovery tasks spanning catalysis, materials science, and organic synthesis.

sections.title: 13 Bayesian Optimisation Algorithm
sections.content: Here, we present compact description of the basic BO workﬂow. The algorithm receives an optimisation problem, including the objective domain (i.e., the design space) and an objective evaluator (e.g., manual experimentation or an external interface), and returns the best solution found. We ﬁrst introduce the overall workﬂow and then discuss key considerations for the involved sub-modules. 13.1 Algorithmic Workﬂow The Bayesian optimisation workﬂow proceeds as follows. The algorithm begins by selecting set of initial observations to construct an initial dataset. At each iteration, the surrogate model is updated with all available data, and the acquisition function is constructed based on the surrogates predictions and associated uncertainties. The acquisition function is then maximised to propose the next candidate point, which is subsequently evaluated using the true objective function (e.g., through experiment or simulation). The new observation is added to the dataset, and this process repeats until stopping criterion is met. Finally, the algorithm recommends solution based on the accumulated data and/or the surrogate model. This workﬂow is summarised in Algorithm 1, and the main optimisation loop is illustrated in Figure 8. Algorithm 1 Bayesian Optimisation (Basic) Require: Design space , objective evaluator evaluate(f, x) 1: Choose ﬁnite subset xinit = (x1, . . . , xn_init) (cid:26) for initial observations. 2: Evaluate yinit evaluate(f, xinit) 3: Construct dataset f(xi, yi)gn_init 4: repeat 5: 6: 7: 8: 9: 10: until Stopping criteria are satisﬁed 11: Recommended ﬁnal solution from and M(ˆf jˆx, D). 12: return Fit surrogate model M(ˆf jˆx, D) from data Construct acquisition function α(x D) from and the surrogate M(ˆf jˆx, D) Select the next point by maximising the acquisition function: arg maxxX α(x D) Evaluate the objective function: evaluate(f, x) Augment data: [ f(x, y)g i= It is important to note that the above describes the basic version of Bayesian optimisation. Numerous extensions exist that modify this workﬂow, such as batched or parallel acquisition (selecting multiple points per iteration), human-inthe-loop interaction (where humans guide or modify the search), or the use of more sophisticated surrogate models

sections.title: BO Tutorial
sections.content: Figure 8: Bayesian Optimisation Loop: At round , surrogate model (e.g., Gaussian process) is ﬁtted to the observed data points (fxt, ytgN t=1). The next point xN +1 is selected by optimising an acquisition function derived from the model posterior. The new observation yN +1 is then evaluated and added to the dataset, iteratively reﬁning the model. The solid brown line depicts the mean prediction of the model, while the dashed lines illustrate the associated variance at each input point x. and acquisition strategies. These extensions can further enhance the efﬁciency and ﬂexibility of Bayesian optimisation in various applications. 13.2 Consideration of Sub-Modules The BO workﬂow described above comprises several key sub-modules [39]. The two central componentsthe surrogate model and the acquisition functionhave been introduced in Part II and Part III. In addition, several auxiliary sub-modules are essential for complete BO implementation, as outlined below. The strategy for choosing initial observations Before entering the main optimisation loop, it is necessary to initialise the dataset with set of initial observations. This is because the surrogate model requires data to build its initial estimate of the objective function. While it is theoretically possible to start with an empty dataset if the surrogate model has well-deﬁned prior, this is uncommon in practice. Typically, initial observations are collected to better inform the model and avoid poor early decisions. The simplest strategy is to randomly sample small number of points from the design space (e.g., uniform sampling). More sophisticated approaches include space-ﬁlling designs [42], such as Latin hypercube sampling, which aim to cover the design space more evenly. Alternatively, expert or human suggestions can be used to select initial points, especially when prior knowledge about promising regions is available. The maximisation tool for the acquisition function At each iteration, the acquisition function must be maximised to determine the next point to evaluatethis is often referred to as the inner optimisation problem [102]. The choice of maximisation method depends on the nature of the design space. For small, ﬁnite spaces, exhaustive search is feasible and guarantees the optimal value. For low-dimensional continuous spaces, discretising the space into ﬁnite grid can be effective. When the acquisition function is differentiable (as is the case with certain surrogates), gradient-based numerical optimisation methods [68] (often with multiple restarts to improve global search) can efﬁciently locate maxima. For more complex or mixed-variable spaces (e.g., combinations of discrete and continuous variables) or for

sections.title: BO Tutorial
sections.content: multi-objective acquisition, heuristic search methods such as evolutionary algorithms or random search are commonly employed [15]. The stopping criteria The optimisation process must be terminated at an appropriate point, and various stopping criteria can be applied. The simplest and most widely-used approach is to specify ﬁxed budget, such as maximum number of iterations or function evaluations. More sophisticated criteria can be designed based on the observed data, the surrogate model, or the acquisition function. For instance, data-based criterion might monitor the empirical improvement over recent steps and halt if the improvement falls below threshold, indicating convergence. model-based criterion could measure the surrogate models uncertainty and stop when the model becomes sufﬁciently conﬁdent. Acquisition-based criteria might terminate the loop if the maximum value of the acquisition function drops below certain threshold, signalling diminishing returns. In more advanced settings, the decision of whether to continue can itself be formalised using Bayesian decision theory [16]. The ﬁnal recommendation Upon termination of the optimisation loop, the algorithm must recommend ﬁnal solution. The most common approach is to select the best observed point in the data (i.e., the input with the highest or lowest observed objective value, depending on the task). Alternatively, the surrogate model can be used to predict the optimum (e.g., the input with the best predicted mean), which may be beneﬁcial if the observations are noisy or sparse.

sections.title: 14 The GP-UCB Algorithm and Theoretical Guarantees
sections.content: In this section, we present canonical and widely studied instance of the Bayesian Optimisation (BO) algorithm: GP-UCB. This algorithm employs Gaussian process (GP) as the surrogate model (see Section 7) and the Upper Conﬁdence Bound (UCB) as the acquisition function (see Section 10.3). GP-UCB is not only standard baseline in practice, but also the subject of extensive theoretical analysis, providing strong regret guarantees for BO [91]. We ﬁrst describe the algorithm and its implementation, then summarise its theoretical guarantees. 14.1 GP-UCB Algorithm The GP-UCB algorithm assumes that the black-box objective function (x) is drawn from Gaussian process prior with mean function m(x) and covariance function k(x, x), and that observations are corrupted by independent, identically distributed (i.i.d.) Gaussian noise. That is, for each query xt, we observe yt = (xt) + ϵt with ϵt (cid:24) (0, σ2). Given history of decisions = (x1, . . . , xt) and their corresponding (possibly noisy) observations = (y1, . . . , yt), the posterior predictive distribution for new candidate ˆx is m(ˆx) + kˆxx[Kxx + σ2I]1(y (cid:0) m), k(ˆx, ˆx) (cid:0) kˆxx[Kxx + σ2I]1kxˆx , (26) (cid:1) M( ˆf jˆx; x, y) = where: (cid:0) kˆxx = [k(ˆx, x1), . . . , k(ˆx, xt)] is the row vector of covariances between ˆx and observed points, and kxˆx is its transpose. Kxx is the (cid:2) covariance matrix with [Kxx]ij = k(xi, xj), = [m(x1), . . . , m(xt)]. The posterior mean and variance are thus: µ(ˆxjx, y) = m(ˆx) + kˆxx[Kxx + σ2I]1(y (cid:0) m) σ2(ˆxjx, y) = k(ˆx, ˆx) (cid:0) kˆxx[Kxx + σ2I]1kxˆx (27) (28) The core idea of GP-UCB is to select, at each iteration, the next point by maximising an upper conﬁdence bound constructed from the GP posterior: (29) where βt (cid:21) 0 is an exploration coefﬁcient, which may be ﬁxed or adapted over time. The algorithm is typically initialised with an empty dataset (or small set of initial observations) and proceeds for ﬁxed budget of iterations, with dynamically chosen βt.

Theorems 14.1 and 14.2 show that, with high probability, the cumulative regret RT of GP-UCB grows at most as O( βT γT ). The information-gain term γT depends on the kernel and the complexity of the underlying function . For commonly used kernels (e.g., squared exponential), γT grows sublinearly with , so the average regret per step RT /T ! 0 as ! 1. This establishes that GP-UCB is no-regret: the algorithm eventually identiﬁes near-optimal solutions, and the search becomes increasingly efﬁcient over time. The choice of the exploration parameter βt plays crucial role: it must be large enough to ensure sufﬁcient exploration (and thus guarantee the regret bounds), but not so large as to excessively prioritise exploration over exploitation. The theoretically derived schedules for βt (as above) are sufﬁcient for regret guarantees, but in practice, βt is often treated as tunable hyperparameter and may be set heuristically or via cross-validation. In summary, GP-UCB provides both practical and theoretically justiﬁed approach to Bayesian Optimisation, balancing exploration and exploitation in principled manner and enjoying strong guarantees on its optimisation performance.

sections.title: 15 Review of Technical Extensions for Scientiﬁc Discovery
sections.content: Optimisation problems encountered in scientiﬁc discovery are often characterised by diverse challenges, rendering the implementation of Bayesian Optimisation (BO) indirect and non-trivial. In this section, we review technical extensions that are particularly valuable for scientiﬁc discovery, focusing on batched decision making, handling heteroscedasticity and non-stationarity, contextual decision making, and integrating human expertise within the optimisation loop. 15.1 Batched Decision Making for High-Throughput Experiments In numerous scientiﬁc and engineering applications, evaluating multiple candidate designs in parallel is feasible and, in many cases, desirable. This scenarioreferred to as batched or parallel Bayesian optimisationnaturally arises in high-throughput experimental platforms. By selecting batch of points to evaluate in each round, rather than single point, one can utilise available resources more efﬁciently and accelerate the overall optimisation process. The central challenge in batched Bayesian optimisation is to select set of candidates, fxt,1, . . . , xt,qg, that collectively offer maximal value for the optimisation objective. Ideally, the batch should strike balance between (i) exploiting the current surrogate model to sample promising candidates, and (ii) exploring uncertain regions to improve the global model. Moreover, the batch should avoid redundant or highly correlated points, ensuring that each experiment yields complementary information. Batched Acquisition Functions natural approach is to develop batched acquisition function α(ˆxjD) for an arbitrary batch ˆx = (ˆx1, . . . , ˆxq) 2 of decision points. Extending standard acquisition functions to the batched setting is, however, non-trivial, as the value of evaluating set of points jointly is not, in general, the sum of their individual acquisition values. For instance, the q-Expected Improvement (q-EI) [28, 27] generalises the standard expected improvement to batches by considering the expected improvement from the best value among the new observations, taking into account their joint distribution under the GP posterior. Nevertheless, q-EI becomes intractable for > 2 and typically requires approximate inference [12]. Other notable batched acquisition functions include the parallel knowledge gradient [105] and batched entropy search [80], which directly account for the information gain or expected utility from evaluating set of points together. These approaches are generally more computationally demanding than their sequential counterparts, but can yield more informative batches. Batching via Acquisition Maximisation Alternatively, batching can be achieved by employing single-point acquisition function, where batching is implemented through maximisation of the acquisition function. One practical approach is to generate batch by optimising the acquisition function multiple times, each time conditioning on the pending (fantasised) outcomes of previous batch members [88, 31]. Recent methods, such as HEBO [15], use population-based optimisers (e.g., evolutionary algorithms) to maximise the acquisition function over the input space, naturally producing diverse set of high-acquisition candidates. This approach is particularly well-suited to highdimensional or mixed-value design spaces. 15.2 Heteroscedasticity and Non-Stationarity Traditional BO often assumes simple i.i.d. Gaussian noise and stationary covariance for uncertainty estimation, particularly when using Gaussian process surrogate function. These assumptions are known as homoscedasticity and stationarity. Homoscedasticity and Heteroscedasticity Homoscedasticity denotes that the noise variance in the observations is constant across all input locations. Formally, if yi = (xi) + ϵi, then Var[ϵi] = σ2 for all i, where σ2 is constant. In contrast, heteroscedasticity refers to the case where the noise variance depends on the input, i.e., Var[ϵi] = σ2(xi). This implies that some regions of the input space are observed with more (or less) noise than others. Stationary and Non-Stationary Covariance Functions kernel or covariance function k(x, x) is termed stationary if it depends solely on the difference between its inputs, i.e., (cid:0) x. This suggests that the statistical properties of the process (such as smoothness or variability) are uniform throughout the input space. Conversely, if the kernel depends on the absolute location or exhibits properties that vary across the input space, it is described as non-stationary. In actual scientiﬁc experiments, the assumptions of homoscedasticity and stationarity are often violated. Heteroscedasticity is common; for example, measurement noise may increase at the boundaries of an instruments range, or depend on the quality of the sample being measured. Non-stationarity frequently arises when the underlying system exhibits different behaviours in various regions of the input spacesuch as phase transitions in materials, or abrupt changes

sections.title: BO Tutorial
sections.content: in biological response. Neglecting these phenomena can result in inaccurate uncertainty estimates and suboptimal decisions in Bayesian optimisation. HEBO [15] is state-of-the-art BO algorithm that systematically addresses heteroscedasticity and non-stationarity. HEBO incorporates concepts from warped Gaussian processes [86], where output transformations help capture complex noise structures. Speciﬁcally, HEBO applies well-established output transformations such as Box-Cox [8] and Yeo-Johnson [110], together with the Kumaraswamy input transformation [49], achieving an effective balance between ease of implementation and strong empirical performance. 15.3 Contextual Decision Making In many real-world scientiﬁc experiments, not all factors inﬂuencing the outcome can be directly controlled by the experimenter. Some variablessuch as environmental conditions, operator effects, or batch-to-batch variabilitymay be observed but not manipulated. This scenario is captured by contextual black-box optimisation. Deﬁnition 15.1 (Contextual Optimisation Problem). Suppose the objective function depends on two sets of variables: controllable design variables 2 , and uncontrollable but observable context variables 2 C. The aim is to ﬁnd, for each context c, design that maximises (or minimises) the contextual objective function (x, c): x(c) = arg max xX (x, c). In practice, is revealed at the time of decision-making, and the experimenter chooses accordingly. Contextual optimisation naturally arises in scientiﬁc discovery. For example, in materials science, ambient temperature or humidity (context) may ﬂuctuate and affect measurements, but only the synthesis parameters (design) can be set. In biology, patient-speciﬁc factors or cell line differences may be observed but not controlled. Properly accounting for such contextual information enables more robust and adaptive experimental strategies. The general solution to contextual Bayesian optimisation is straightforward: Given context c, one ﬁrst establishes the contextual surrogate model M(ˆf jˆx, c, D) and then constructs the contextual acquisition function α(ˆxjD, c) based on this model. Krause and Ong [44] provides theoretical analysis of such contextual extensions to GP-UCB, referred to as CGP-UCB, where denotes contextual. In this approach, Gaussian process is ﬁtted directly on the joint space of design-context pairs and then used as the surrogate model by ﬁxing the context. Recently, Williams and Rasmussen [101] sought to better model the complex synergy between context and decision by aggregating the outputs of multioutput Gaussian process (MOGP) over , where the weights of outputs are produced from the context via neural network. 15.4 Integrating Human Expertise in the Optimisation Loop In many scientiﬁc domains, expert knowledge is crucial for guiding experimental design and interpretation. While it is, in principle, possible to encode such expert knowledge into the BO frameworkthrough surrogate model architecture, prior distributions, or initialisation strategiespractical implementation can be extremely challenging and usually requires strong background in Machine Learning and Statistics. Expert intuition is often tacit, context-dependent, or difﬁcult to formalise mathematically. Consequently, fully automated BO may fail to leverage valuable human insights, or may make decisions that the domain expert ﬁnds implausible or untrustworthy. In real-world discovery, full automation may not be necessity. Instead, it is often reasonable to allow human experts to intervene directly in the optimisation loop [46]. This approach maintains scientiﬁc trust and harnesses domain expertise ﬂexibly, without requiring all knowledge to be pre-engineered into the algorithm. There are several ways to integrate human intervention into the BO process. One straightforward approach is to allow the expert to override or reject the candidate design suggested by the algorithm at each iteration. Alternatively, the acquisition function can propose batch of promising candidates, from which the expert selects the most appropriate one according to their own preferences [77]. Human intervention can be applied at every iteration, only when the models uncertainty is high, or when the acquisition function is very ﬂat and multiple candidates are equally plausible. This ﬂexibility allows the optimisation process to adapt to the needs of the application and the preferences of the scientiﬁc team. In Algorithm 3, we modify the standard BO pseudocode to illustrate how human-in-the-loop decision-making can be incorporated as simple variant of the basic BO workﬂow. This human-in-the-loop approach enables the optimisation process to beneﬁt from both the data-driven guidance of BO and the nuanced judgement of domain experts. Such workﬂow is extremely straightforward to implement at the code level, especially for natural scientists without strong background in computer science.

sections.content: Algorithm 3 Bayesian Optimisation with Human-in-the-Loop Require: Design space , objective evaluator evaluate(f, x): 1: Choose ﬁnite subset xinit = (x1, . . . , xn_init) (cid:26) for initial observations, possibly by humans 2: Evaluate yinit evaluate(f, xinit) 3: Construct dataset f(xi, yi)gn_init 4: repeat 5: 6: 7: 8: 9: Fit surrogate model M(ˆf jˆx, D) from data Construct acquisition function α(x D) from and the surrogate if human intervention is needed then Propose set of candidate points Xcand (e.g., top by acquisition value) Expert reviews Xcand, and either selects point to evaluate, or overrides/rejects all candidates and suggests an alternative i=1 else arg maxx α(x D) end if Evaluate the objective function: evaluate(f, x) Augment data: [ f(x, y)g 10: 11: 12: 13: 14: 15: until Stopping criteria are satisﬁed 16: Recommended ﬁnal solution from and M. 17: return Regarding research advances in human-in-the-loop BO, certain approaches [1, 108] employ preference learning or expert belief modelling. Recent studies have also investigated whether human experts can be replaced by large language models [13], which is promising for fully-automated knowledge integration.

sections.title: 16 Coding BO Workﬂows
sections.content: In this section, we present BO implementations at the code level. First, we show how to implement BO using an existing BO library such as HEBO, where both standard and human-in-the-loop variants are illustrated. Then, we build custom workﬂows with Gaussian Process surrogates and acquisition functions. 16.1 Coding the Workﬂow with HEBO In this section, we demonstrate how to implement the Bayesian optimisation (BO) workﬂow using the HEBO library. HEBO (Heteroscedastic Evolutionary Bayesian Optimisation) is state-of-the-art BO framework that handles mixedvariable design spaces, supports parallel suggestions, and addresses heteroscedasticity and non-stationarity through input and output transformations [15]. We recall Section 4 and assume the optimisation problem is already deﬁned with design space and an evaluation interface as follows: space: an instance of DesignSpace from HEBO, which encapsulates the parameter deﬁnitions (continuous, integer, categorical, etc.). evaluate(xs): function that takes pandas DataFrame of design points (one per row) and returns NumPy array of corresponding objective values (to be maximised). Because HEBO is designed for minimisation, we maximise by observing the negative of the original objective. 16.1.1 General Workﬂow Using HEBO The basic BO loop proceeds according to the structure introduced in Algorithm 1. The key components are implemented as follows: Initialisation: HEBO automatically performs an initial random exploration phase. The number of random samples is controlled by the rand_sample argument; if not speciﬁed, it defaults to 1 + number of parameters. This corresponds to the "initial observations" step. Surrogate model and acquisition function: These are internal to HEBO. Each call to suggest ﬁts the surrogate (a Gaussian process by default) to all available data, constructs an acquisition function (e.g., MACE), and maximises it to propose the next candidate(s).

This hybrid workﬂow retains the data-driven guidance of BO while allowing domain knowledge to override or reﬁne algorithmic suggestions. The same BO components as above are used, but the candidate-selection step is replaced by an interactive human decision. The loop continues until the budget is exhausted, and the ﬁnal recommendation remains the best observed point. The implementation extends the standard loop by generating batch of batch_size candidates, displaying them with their indices, and prompting the user to enter the index of the chosen point. Input validation ensures valid selection. The selected point is then evaluated and fed back to the optimiser. = 30 batch_size = 5 opt = HEBO ( space , rand_sample =10 , scramble_seed =42) for in range ( ) : candidates = opt . suggest ( n_suggestions = batch_size ) print ( " nCandidates ( index ) : " , candidates . to_string ( index = True ) ) # Get valid input from human while True : try : choice = int ( input ( " Select index : " ) ) if 0 <= choice < len ( candidates ) : break else : print ( " Index must be between 0 and { len ( candidates ) -1}. [ best_idx ][0] print ( " nBest design : " , best_x ) print ( " Best objective : { best_y :.4 } " ) 16.2 Coding the Workﬂow from Customised Surrogate and Acquisition Function In this section, we implement complete Bayesian optimisation (BO) workﬂow by integrating the Gaussian process (GP) surrogate models from Section 9.2 and the acquisition functions from Section 12.2 into full optimisation loop. We use the Upper Conﬁdence Bound (UCB) acquisition function as working example, with the exploration parameter set to β = 2.0, and initialise the GP with Matérn kernel. The same loop structure, however, directly applies to other acquisition functions such as Expected Improvement (EI) and Probability of Improvement (PI). For illustration, we consider two-dimensional design space = [0, 1]2. At each BO iteration, we maximise the acquisition function via grid search over dense mesh of candidate points. This strategy is practical for low-dimensional problems and provides transparent demonstration of the BO workﬂow. As synthetic test problem, we optimise the function (x1, x2) = sin(5πx1) cos(5πx2) + 0.5 cos(10πx1) sin(10πx2), which is wavy function with multiple local optima in two dimensions. In real application, this synthetic objective and the 2D grid would be replaced by the actual black-box objective (e.g., an experimental measurement or simulation) and the true design space (which may be high-dimensional, mixed, or constrained). The overall structure of the loop remains unchanged and follows Algorithm 1: Initialisation: We randomly sample few points from the design space to form the initial dataset. Here we use simple random design; more sophisticated spaceﬁlling designs could be substituted. Surrogate update: At each iteration, GP is ﬁtted to all available data. Acquisition maximisation: We create ﬁne mesh of candidate points in [0, 1]2, compute the UCB value for each, and select the point with the highest acquisition. Evaluation: The chosen point is evaluated using the true objective function (here synthetic test function). The new observation is added to the dataset. Termination: ﬁxed budget of iterations is used as the stopping criterion. Final recommendation: After the loop, we return the best observed point (the one with the highest objective value, since we are maximising). Remark on Practical Use. The implementations in this section are intentionally minimal and serve solely to illustrate the core concepts of Bayesian optimisation with custom surrogate models and acquisition functions. In real application, the synthetic test function and the two-dimensional grid would be replaced by the actual black-box objective (e.g., an experimental measurement or simulation) and the true design space (which may be high-dimensional, mixed, or constrained). The structure of the loop, however, remains unchanged. Production-grade BO often requires additional features such as parallel (batched) evaluations, support for mixed continuous/discrete spaces, sophisticated acquisition optimisation, and robust handling of noise and non-stationarity. Incorporating these elements from scratch would require signiﬁcant amount of code and external libraries. For real-world applications, we recommend leveraging established libraries such as HEBO [15], BoTorch [3], or BayesOpt/GPy [69], which encapsulate these complexities and offer well-tested, scalable implementations.

The model is retrained from scratch at each iteration; in practice one could warmstart the hyperparameters, but for clarity we keep the loop simple. The acquisition maximisation again uses dense grid, which is straightforward in 2D. Integrating BO with Scientiﬁc Tools In many scientiﬁc domains, optimisation must be performed over non-conventional spaces, such as molecules, which are typically represented by SMILES strings rather than standard numerical vectors. This presents unique challenges for Bayesian Optimisation (BO), as standard surrogate models and acquisition functions are designed for vector spaces. To address this, domain-speciﬁc feature extraction is often employed to map complex scientiﬁc objects (e.g., molecules, proteins, graphs) into vector space suitable for surrogate modelling. 17.1 Example: Molecular Descriptors for QED Optimisation Recalling the example in Section 5.5, we consider the problem of molecular optimisation. The search space is restricted to large but ﬁnite set of candidate molecules, each represented by SMILES string. The objective function is blackbox performance score of each molecule. As an illustrative example, we consider QED [7] as the molecular scoring function. We follow the Practical Molecule Optimisation benchmark [23], deﬁning as subset of nearly 250,000 molecules represented by SMILES strings extracted from ZINC. To enable BO in this discrete, structured space, we employ molecular descriptors provided by RDKit [75] to construct feature map. Speciﬁcally, each SMILES string is converted into vector of chemically meaningful descriptors, including molecular weight, logP, number of hydrogen bond donors/acceptors, topological polar surface area, etc. This feature vector serves as the input to the surrogate model, allowing the GP to operate in continuous vector space while retaining the underlying chemical information. Speciﬁcally, the BO workﬂow is adapted as follows: Surrogate Model: Gaussian process (GP) is used as the surrogate, but instead of operating directly on SMILES strings, it models the relationship between molecular descriptor vectors and the QED score. Acquisition Function: The Upper Conﬁdence Bound (UCB) acquisition function is constructed using the GPs posterior mean and variance over the descriptor space. Acquisition Optimisation: Since the search space is large and discrete, we sample subset of candidate molecules at each iteration, compute their descriptors, and select the top candidates according to the acquisition function.

Instead, we demonstrate ﬂexible and transparent approach by implementing the workﬂow from scratch using GPyTorch for the Gaussian Process surrogate, and RDKit for feature extraction. Here, each molecule (represented as SMILES string) is mapped to vector of chemical descriptors, enabling the GP to operate in continuous latent space.
